narrativeSummary: Version 3.33.0 is a major consolidation release: ~5 weeks of in-progress work has been drained from a local WIP stash and shipped together. The Activity Editor finance area (Budgets, Planned Disbursements, Conditions, Linked Transactions, Loan Terms) is rebuilt with type-safe schemas. Every list page now has a unified Export button (Filtered / All × CSV / Excel) and an optimistic-delete + undo-toast pattern. Government Financial Contributions, the Readiness Wizard, and the Aid Effectiveness Form have been rebuilt. The Activity list gains a separate IATI ID column with click-to-copy chips. A new gunmetal palette + slightly cooler primary token modernise the look. Documents, Locations, and Working Groups tabs receive UX polish, and 14 new IATI 2.03 codelist tables are added as the foundation for upcoming compliance work.

Contributions are now captured as a list of typed entries, each rendered as a bordered table row inside the Government Financial Contribution card
Columns: Type (badge + sub-label) / Description / Original Value (currency prefixed in small gray) / USD Value (monochrome, not green) / actions

Financial Contribution Form
Field order reworked: Value / Currency → Value Date / Exchange Rate → USD Value → Description
Value input uses formatted thousands (1,000,000) matching the budget modal
Exchange Rate has an inline Auto / Manual toggle with Switch; auto-fetched from historical rates based on value date
USD Value is read-only, monochrome
Description is a resizable <Textarea>
Distribution Mode is now two illustrated cards (Lump Sum — whale; Annual Breakdown — salmon) with Check badge on the selected one
Annual Breakdown — per-year accuracy
Top-level Value Date + Exchange Rate are hidden in Annual mode (each year now has its own anchor)
Each row has: Year / Value Date (DatePicker, defaults to FY start) / Amount (formatted thousands) / Rate (per-year, auto-fetched) / USD Value / delete
Top-level USD Value becomes the sum of per-year USD amounts
Per-row rates are fetched in parallel when the value date changes; rows flagged Manual keep their entered rate
Breakdown period is editable: Breakdown start + Breakdown end DatePickers default to activity planned dates but can be overridden for gov schedules that extend beyond the activity
"Generate Years" respects the breakdown period; existing year amounts are preserved on regenerate

The clear (×) control was a <button> nested inside the PopoverTrigger <button>, which is invalid HTML and caused React hydration warnings plus silently swallowed clicks on the trigger in some states

releases.narrativeSummary: This release focuses on map usability, select component polish, and Activity Editor scroll reliability. The Activity Locations Map controls (tile selector, markers/heatmap toggle, 3D view, reset, zoom, fullscreen, and export) are all moved from an overlay on top of the map to a dedicated toolbar row above it, so controls never obscure the map content. The global Select component now places checkmarks on the right side of options instead of the left. The IATI Select and hierarchical admin selector follow the same pattern. All six Activity Editor section groups get smarter scroll-to-section logic that detects whether layout shifts actually displaced the target and only re-scrolls when needed, replacing the old blind 800ms timeout. Several CheckCircle2 icons have been swapped to CheckCircle for visual consistency across the Activity Editor tabs.
changes: Activity Locations Map — Controls Moved to Toolbar
Map tile selector, markers/heatmap toggle, and export button now sit in a toolbar row above the map instead of overlaying it
3D/Reset and zoom/fullscreen controls also moved to the toolbar using React portals (they need map context internally)
The map surface is now completely unobstructed — no floating controls
Location Modal map: 3D toggle and reset buttons compacted; map-internal 3D controller removed in favour of toolbar buttons
Select Component — Right-Side Checkmarks
The global Select component now shows the selected-item checkmark on the right side instead of the left
Left padding reduced and right padding added to accommodate the new layout
IATI Select dropdown: checkmark moved from the left margin to inline on the right of the option text
Hierarchical admin select (regions/townships): same right-side checkmark treatment
IATI Select — Capitalised Option Names
Option names in SelectIATI now use capitalize for cleaner readability
Activity Editor — Smarter Scroll-to-Section
All six section groups (Overview, Stakeholders, Funding & Delivery, Strategic Alignment, Supporting Info, Advanced) now use layout-shift-aware re-scroll logic
Instead of blindly re-scrolling after 800ms, the system measures whether the target element actually moved and only re-scrolls if it shifted by more than 5px
Scroll-spy root margin tightened from 60% to 50% with debounce increased from 100ms to 150ms for more stable section tracking
Location Card — Saved Indicator Moved
The green checkmark indicating a saved location now appears next to the location name instead of next to the description

Date description popover triggers in the New Activity form now always show a pencil icon with neutral styling, removing the conditional sticky-note icon
Icon Consistency
Replaced CheckCircle2 with CheckCircle across Comments, Compare Data, Results, and Indicator components for a consistent single-ring style

releases.narrativeSummary: This release adds a complete Visitor Mode for unauthenticated read-only browsing, a new My Team dashboard tab, redesigned hero cards with activity lifecycle breakdowns, a visual year-grid calendar picker, optimised authentication, illustrated empty states, and dozens of smaller improvements across the platform. Over 130 files were changed, including 30+ API routes updated to support visitor access and a new middleware layer for visitor route protection.
changes: Visitor Mode — Browse Without Signing In
New “Browse as Visitor” button on the login page lets anyone explore the platform read-only

Client-side defence-in-depth blocks all non-GET API calls for visitors
30+ API routes updated to use requireAuthOrVisitor() for read-only visitor access via admin client
Personal dashboard tabs (My Portfolio, My Team, Notifications, Bookmarks, Tasking) hidden for visitors
New VISITOR role with zero permissions in the role/permissions system
Dashboard — My Team Tab

Card and table view modes with sorting by name, role, and email
Search filtering to quickly find team members

Transactions card now shows “Your Organisation” vs “Other Organisations” (transactions where you are provider/receiver on other org’s activities)

New activity lifecycle breakdown card showing Pipeline, Implementation, Finalisation, Closed, Cancelled, and Suspended counts with colour-coded progress bars

Calendar Year Picker — Visual Grid

Authentication — Optimised

Empty States — Illustrated

releases.narrativeSummary: This is the biggest UI consistency pass in the project’s history, touching over 230 files across every module. The goal: make every table sortable, every colour semantic, every code badge uniform, and every page title consistent. Under the hood, 13 duplicate currency-formatting functions have been collapsed into one canonical export, border radii have been tightened from 8px to 12px for a more modern feel, and a new --success-icon CSS variable ensures green icons match the semantic success palette everywhere. Sortable columns have been added to the Organisation Activities, Budgets, Planned Disbursements, Transactions, Overdue Tasks, and Top Performers tables. The sector chart colour palette has been harmonised with the dashboard’s neutral tones.
changes: Sortable Tables — Everywhere
Organisation Activities table: sort by title, activity status, publication status, validation status, budget, planned disbursements, last updated, end date, and days remaining
Organisation Budgets and Planned Disbursements tables: full column sorting
Organisation Transactions table: sortable columns added
Task admin tables (Overdue Tracking, Top Performers): sort by task, type, priority, days overdue, progress, and creator
New shared utilities (getSortIcon, sortableHeaderClasses) in the Table component for consistent sort indicators
Semantic Colour System
All hardcoded text-green-500, bg-green-100, text-green-700 etc. replaced with semantic CSS variables: --success-icon, --success-bg, --success-text, --success-border
Same treatment for warning, error, and info colour families
Badge variants updated to use CSS variables instead of hardcoded Tailwind classes
Validation status labels, notification icons, focal point checks, task status indicators, and save confirmations all updated
Unified CodeBadge Component
New reusable CodeBadge component for all IATI identifiers, sector codes, location codes, and organisation IDs
Consistent font-mono text-xs bg-muted styling across the entire platform
Removed inconsistent font-bold and text-[10px] sizing from individual pages
Typography & Layout Consistency
All H1 page titles standardised to text-3xl font-bold (dashboard, SDG detail, sector detail, policy markers)
MiniChartCard titles standardised from text-xs font-semibold to text-sm font-medium
Policy markers inline chart titles updated to match the new pattern
Currency Formatting — Consolidated
13 duplicate formatCurrencyShort definitions across the codebase replaced with a single canonical export in lib/format.ts
All pages now import from one source — no more drift between formatting styles
Border Radius Refinement
Global --radius changed from 0.5rem (8px) to 0.75rem (12px) for a more modern, rounded feel
Medium (6px) and small (4px) radii hardcoded to preserve their values
Sector Chart Palette
Sector distribution chart colours updated from mixed bright tones to a harmonised neutral palette matching the dashboard’s design language
Working Groups — Members Section
Members section updated with improved layout and styling consistency

releases.narrativeSummary: This release transforms the dashboard into a proper financial command centre. Organisation data is now presented through a tabbed interface covering Activities, Transactions, Budgets, and Planned Disbursements — each with full pagination and sorting. Bookmarked activities and organisations gain card and table view toggles with sortable columns. The activity calendar heatmap gets click-to-pin tooltips so you can interact with the detail popover. The task system receives substantial improvements: the creation wizard now lets you rename attachments inline, validates fields only after you attempt to proceed, and all task API routes use admin-level database access for reliable cross-user operations. Notifications gain an Active/Archived toggle with full unarchive support. The My Portfolio tab now links missing-data items directly to their activities, and activity edits are tracked with a dedicated last_edited_by column for precise attribution on recency cards.
changes: Dashboard — Unified Financial Data Hub

New dedicated Budgets table showing period dates, amounts, currency, status, and linked activity
New dedicated Planned Disbursements table with provider/receiver organisations and USD conversion
Removed the standalone Activities tab from the main dashboard navigation for a cleaner layout

Table columns are sortable by title, reporting organisation, and last updated date
Bookmarked Organisations table gains sortable columns and a card/table toggle

Validation errors now only appear after you attempt to advance past a step, not while you’re still filling it in
Task creation error messages now surface the actual server error instead of a generic failure
All task API routes switched to admin-level Supabase client for reliable cross-user operations
Notifications — Archive & Unarchive

My Portfolio — Linked Missing-Data Alerts

Recency Cards — Accurate Edit Attribution

The “Last Edited” recency card now correctly shows who made the most recent edit
Legacy activities without an editor recorded gracefully fall back to showing “you”

Each card shows both organisation-wide and personal (“By you”) counts
Clickable navigation removed from hero cards for a cleaner, information-first layout
Chart Visualisations — Enhanced
All dashboard charts now have a CSV export button for downloading data
Sector distribution chart now supports three view modes: horizontal bar, vertical bar, and table
Transaction bars changed from stacked to grouped for clearer comparison

Fixed “last edited activity” dashboard card crashing when no activities have been edited (null updated_at handling)
Fixed profile banner save race condition with versioned save tracking
Fixed SDG profile page validation logic to prevent unnecessary API calls

Simplified organisation display on the dashboard welcome card

releases.narrativeSummary: Consistent, polished profile pages for SDGs, Sectors, and Locations with banner images, unified colour palette, expandable charts, and streamlined layouts.

Consistent breadcrumb navigation across all profile types

releases.narrativeSummary: A polished welcome experience for first-time users, a refreshed Home page, major Working Groups improvements, and quality-of-life fixes across the platform.

releases.narrativeSummary: This release redesigns the Government Inputs section to eliminate duplicate data entry and adds intelligent automation to the Readiness Checklist. The Coordination tab is removed (data is already captured in Participating Organizations, Contacts, and Locations). The Oversight tab is replaced with a scored Risk Assessment matrix featuring 10 questions across 5 categories (Political, Environmental, Social, Fiduciary, Operational) with color-coded summary badges. Oversight requirements become auto-check items in the Readiness Checklist that verify live data. A new drag-and-drop document upload component supports multiple files across Budget Classification, Agreement, and Evaluation sections. Budget classification dimension cards now display help descriptions inline below each label.

Document Uploads — Drag & Drop

releases.narrativeSummary: This release refines the Activity Editor experience across many sections. The focal point assignment modal is rebuilt with inline user search and preloaded results, replacing the previous dropdown select. Linked activities search is similarly upgraded with preloaded activity lists and collapsible relationship groups showing organization logos. The conditions form moves into a proper modal dialog. The disbursement channel selector is rebuilt as a searchable popover command palette. Aid effectiveness indicators get a searchable combobox for national plan selection. Sector allocation charts are collapsed by default behind a toggle. Rich text description fields now autosave with a 3-second debounce instead of saving on every keystroke. The date picker gains an endAdornment prop. Documents display full language names next to codes. Geography and sector mode toggles show skeleton placeholders during switching. Section headers accept an action prop for inline buttons. The countries/regions tab removes redundant cards and headers for a cleaner layout. Policy marker tab completion logic is simplified. Over 30 form select components receive minor consistency fixes.
changes: Focal Point Assignment — Inline Search

FocalPointsTab updated with improved state management and contact search integration

Relationship groups displayed in collapsible sections with GitBranch icons and organization logos

Disbursement Channel — Searchable Popover
Replaced Select dropdown with Popover + Command palette for disbursement channel selection, with checkmark indicators
Aid Effectiveness — Searchable Combobox
National plan indicator selection rebuilt as a searchable combobox with Popover + Command, supporting multiple evidence document uploads per field
Sector Allocation — Collapsed Charts
Sankey visualization section now collapsed by default behind a Collapsible toggle with BarChart2 icon
Rich Text Autosave — Debounced Saves
Description, objectives, target groups, and other narrative fields now autosave with 3-second debounce instead of immediate saves on every keystroke
UI Component Refinements

SectionHeader gains action prop for inline action buttons
Geography and sector mode toggles show skeleton placeholders during switching instead of a spinner

30+ form select components receive minor consistency and spacing fixes

releases.narrativeSummary: This release fixes Activity Editor scroll navigation and loading issues. When editing an activity, clicking sidebar tabs during loading would scroll to the wrong section due to layout shifts caused by small skeleton placeholders being replaced by full-height content. The scroll spy would also highlight the wrong section during these transitions. These issues are resolved through height-reserving skeletons, consolidated scroll navigation, scroll spy locking, and batch section preloading.
changes: Activity Editor — Scroll Navigation Fix
Sidebar tab clicks now reliably scroll to the correct section, even during initial page load
Eliminated duplicate scrollIntoView calls between navigation and group components
Scroll uses requestAnimationFrame to wait for DOM to settle before scrolling
Activity Editor — Layout Shift Prevention
New shared SectionSkeleton component with per-section estimated heights (280–560px) and layout-appropriate skeleton variants (form, table, cards, mixed)
All 24 section containers now reserve minHeight to prevent content-swap jumps
Activity Editor — Scroll Spy Improvements
New lockScrollSpy() function suppresses scroll spy updates for 2 seconds during programmatic navigation
Prevents sidebar highlight from flickering when content loads and shifts the page
Activity Editor — Preloading Optimisation
Replaced staggered setTimeout preloading with single-batch activateSections() calls across all 6 group components
Increased IntersectionObserver preload distance from 800px to 1500px for earlier section loading

releases.narrativeSummary: This release focuses on design-system consistency and component consolidation. Sixty-three hardcoded Tailwind gray colour utilities (text-gray-500, bg-gray-50, border-gray-200, etc.) are replaced with semantic theme tokens (text-muted-foreground, bg-muted, border-border, text-foreground) across 100+ files, enabling proper dark-mode theming. Five new shared UI components are introduced: RequiredDot (red dot for required fields), FilterBar (standardised filter container), ConfirmationDialog (reusable confirm/cancel modal), FormErrorAlert (conditional destructive alert), and ModalFooter (cancel/submit button pair with loading state). These replace dozens of inline implementations. The internal-transfer exclusion filter in analytics is refined to only exclude transactions belonging to pooled-fund activities with activity links, fixing a bug where regular activity transactions were incorrectly filtered from portfolio totals. The fund auto-link suggestion algorithm is extracted from the API route into a standalone utility at lib/fund-auto-link.ts. A new useLocalStorage hook is added for persistent client-side state.
changes: Design System — Theme Token Migration
Replaced 63 hardcoded text-gray-*, bg-gray-*, and border-gray-* classes with semantic tokens (text-foreground, text-muted-foreground, bg-muted, border-border) across 100+ files
Ensures consistent appearance in both light and dark mode themes
New Shared UI Components
RequiredDot — red dot indicator for required form fields, replacing inline <span> elements
FilterBar — standardised filter container with consistent padding, background, and border styling
ConfirmationDialog — reusable confirm/cancel modal with warning icon, customisable title, description, and loading state
FormErrorAlert — conditional destructive alert that renders only when an error string is present
ModalFooter — cancel/submit button pair with loading spinner, adopted across multiple modal forms
Internal Transfer Filter Fix
excludeInternalTransfers() now accepts a pooledFundIds parameter and only excludes transactions belonging to pooled-fund activities
New getPooledFundIds() helper fetches pooled fund IDs once per request
Regular activities with activity links are no longer incorrectly excluded from portfolio analytics totals
All analytics API routes updated to pass pooled fund IDs to the filter
Fund Auto-Link Refactor
Fund suggestion algorithm extracted from api/activities/[id]/fund-suggestions/route.ts into lib/fund-auto-link.ts
API route now calls generateFundSuggestions() utility, reducing route file by ~180 lines
Other Improvements
New useLocalStorage hook for persistent client-side state
Filter bars across pages (activities, budgets, transactions, reports, organisations, etc.) migrated to FilterBar component
Empty state cards use rounded-lg consistently instead of rounded-md
Hover states refined to bg-muted/50 for subtler interaction feedback

releases.narrativeSummary: This release focuses on security hardening, onboarding, and usability improvements. CORS is now restricted to configured origins instead of wildcard, all auth API routes use centralised authentication, and six debug/test endpoints have been removed. A new onboarding modal guides first-time users. The top navigation is rebuilt with shadcn dropdown menus replacing the bloom-menu dependency. Multi-select components gain live search filtering. The Fund Flow Sankey chart displays full donor names with acronyms and wraps long labels. The project list adds a Date Added column and copy-to-clipboard buttons for project names and codes. The transaction table gains an Internal Transfer detection column. Scoring criteria migrations introduce graduated MSDP goal points and expanded intake technical maturity checks including description, objectives, and target beneficiaries fields. Budget classifications now support acronyms.

Scoring Criteria Updates

MSDP Alignment scoring graduates additional goals: 3 pts for 1, 7 pts for 2, 10 pts for 3+ secondary goals

IATI export route adds error handling for missing activity data fields
Profile page improvements and auth callback cleanup
Aid effectiveness options management and budget classifications admin UI refinements

releases.narrativeSummary: This release delivers a comprehensive UI/UX improvement pass across the Project Bank. The Preliminary Feasibility Study form is redesigned with split first/last name fields for conductors, 200-character suggestion counters on all textareas, contextual help tooltips, tag-style revenue source selectors, conditional land acquisition and resettlement detail fields, and MSDP goal image cards with multi-select strategy tags. The Review Board is overhauled with faded banner images on kanban cards, reviewer name badges, sector codes in filter dropdowns fetched from the database, new region and category filters, estimated cost displayed on all cards, and renamed review steps. Project profile pages gain a Documents tab with signed-URL downloads. Financial analysis fixes address field indentation, cash flow net alignment, USD labels, and conditional revenue field visibility.

releases.narrativeSummary: This release introduces Category D (Development Partner / ODA) alongside the existing A/B/C categories, giving the review board a fourth pathway for public-good projects with strong social returns. Three new FS-3 structuring forms replace the single PPP component: Private Investment (Category A), Government Budget (Category B), and ODA Transfer (Category D), each with category-specific fields and procurement options. The appraisal wizard dynamically dispatches the correct form based on the assigned category. Feasibility stage labels throughout the system are renamed from FS-1/FS-2 to PFS (Preliminary Feasibility Study) and DFS (Detailed Feasibility Study). Rejected projects now have a recovery workflow with a modal that lets administrators return them to earlier draft stages. The project detail API parallelises its donor, appraisal, and document fetches for faster page loads. Stage-transition security is tightened so only safe draft transitions are allowed through the general PUT endpoint, and project deletion is restricted to admin roles.

releases.narrativeSummary: This release splits the contact officer and proponent single-name fields into separate first-name and last-name columns, with a database migration that auto-populates existing data. A new Phase 4 Categorized Projects tab appears on the Review Board, grouping projects into Category A (Private Investment), Category B (Government Budget), and Category C (PPP / VGF Structuring) via a Kanban and table view. The MSDP screening form replaces the free-text strategy input with a structured dropdown listing all 28 MSDP strategies grouped by goal, and the sector strategy reference field becomes a multi-select backed by aid-effectiveness options from the admin panel. releases.narrativeSummary: This release introduces the FS-3 PPP / VGF Structuring phase — the fourth and final stage of the feasibility pipeline. Projects reaching Category C can now be submitted, scored, and reviewed through PPP Transaction Structuring. The scoring system gains multi-stage calculation (score all applicable stages at once), a new active-rubric API, and dimension help tooltips that explain sub-criteria and point allocations per phase. A new Economic Analysis Charts component renders EIRR cost/benefit breakdowns and NPV/BCR metrics on the project detail page. Cash flow charts add a bar/line toggle. The appraisal wizard improves prior-phase editing with a Save & Re-lock button, validates required fields on Continue (not just Submit), and routes FS-3 read-only viewing correctly. The project detail page moves the categorization result to the sidebar and removes the standalone Scoring tab in favour of inline score display. Database migrations add PPP structuring columns and FS-3 scoring rubric seed data.

releases.narrativeSummary: This release introduces an automated scoring system that evaluates projects across five weighted dimensions — MSDP alignment, financial viability, technical maturity, environmental and social risk, and institutional capacity. Scores are computed automatically when a project is submitted for review or when a reviewer completes a decision, and they appear as a score card on the project detail page, a composite score column on the project list, and a live score sidebar in the appraisal wizard. Administrators can manage scoring rubrics (criteria, weights, sub-criteria) from a new Scoring Rubric tab in the admin panel. The EIRR calculator has been redesigned with 15 named benefit categories (time savings, agricultural surplus, health benefits, carbon reduction, etc.) in a structured table, replacing the old single-amount-per-category rows. The appraisal wizard now has an unlock button on locked or submitted forms, letting authorised users edit data in prior phases. The project list replaces the old Status and Feasibility columns with a Score column and stacked pipeline stage badges showing Phase, Review tier, and Status at a glance. The FS-2 tabs have been relabelled from Financial/Economic to FIRR/EIRR for clarity. Several UI refinements include tooltip-truncated location descriptions, compact admin action buttons, merged Code/Name columns in budget classifications, and a Zap icon on the Quick Entry button in cash flow tables.

releases.narrativeSummary: This release adds the Detailed Feasibility Study (FS-2) phase, giving consultants an eight-section form to document demand, technical, financial, environmental, risk, and implementation analysis. The review board now uses a three-step claim workflow — Pending, Desk Review, Senior Review — across all phases, and senior reviewers can return items to desk review for re-screening. You can look back at earlier phases (Intake or FS-1) read-only from any later stage. The sidebar links directly to each review board phase, the progress rail shows your project code with click-to-copy, and a validation dialog highlights any missing fields before you submit.

releases.narrativeSummary: This release focuses on making project data more accessible, improving the appraisal workflow, and fixing review board stage handling. The project detail page has been completely restructured: the overview tab now uses a two-column card layout with project metadata on the left (origin, type, PPP contract type, estimated start date, duration, description, objectives, beneficiaries, contact officer, SDGs, MSDP alignment) and financial details on the right (cost breakdown chart, funding progress bar, pathway, categorisation). The collapsible PFS sections have been removed in favour of this cleaner layout. The dashboard “Recent Submissions” table has been expanded from a simple five-row preview to a full data table fetching all projects with eight sortable columns (Project Title, Sector, Ministry/Agency, Project Type, State/Region, Estimated Start Date, Estimated Cost, Status), client-side pagination with configurable page sizes, and sortable column headers with ascending/descending indicators. Rows support middle-click and Ctrl/Cmd-click to open in new tabs. The appraisal wizard now supports read-only intake viewing when working in the FS-1 phase: clicking the “Intake” step in the progress rail shows the intake form greyed out with a yellow “Viewing intake data (read-only)” banner and a “Back to Feasibility Study” button. The viability sidebar and action bar are hidden during intake viewing. The progress rail shows the project name at the top and the FS-1 phase heading is clickable to return to the current phase. The PFS form now displays a validation error summary below the cost table, mapping each error to its tab with clickable links that switch to the relevant tab. The contact officer section has been moved from a standalone section at the bottom of the intake form to an inline card directly under the Project Origin cards, with the proponent section shown for unsolicited proposals. The FS-1 review board API has been fixed to query project_stage instead of feasibility_stage, and the FS-1 review endpoint now reads project_stage for tier validation and maps the “screened” decision to fs1_desk_screened correctly. The submit endpoint now syncs feasibility_stage when submitting from FS-1. The wizard auto-transitions project_stage from intake_approved to fs1_draft on first FS-1 save. Seven new Project Bank badge variants have been added to the design system (pb-entry, pb-progress, pb-review, pb-approved, pb-active, pb-done, pb-rejected) and status badges on the dashboard and project list use these variants. The funding gap hero card now shows the number of projects with gaps. Metadata in review board Kanban cards uses a stacked layout (implementing agency and sub-sector on separate lines) instead of inline dot separators. The funding gaps page now has sortable column headers across all columns. The date picker uses the full date format (e.g. “4 March 2026”). Currency formatting always shows the code prefix (e.g. “USD”) instead of “$”. A seed script provides sample Project Bank data for development environments.

releases.narrativeSummary: This release extends the two-tier review model to all review board phases and adds richer metadata throughout the project pipeline. The Intake Review Board now supports a full desk/senior workflow: desk screeners can pass, return, or reject submissions, and projects that pass desk screening advance to a senior review stage before final approval. A new intake_desk_screened project stage tracks this intermediate state. The FS-2 Review Board has been expanded with three new stages — fs2_desk_reviewed, fs2_senior_reviewed, and fs2_returned — enabling the same desk-then-senior pattern for detailed feasibility studies. The categorisation endpoint now accepts projects from fs2_senior_reviewed in addition to fs2_completed. The Intake Review Board gains a Kanban view with three columns (Desk Review, Senior Review, Returned), complementing the existing card, list, and table views. All review board tabs are now labelled with phase numbers (Phase 1, Phase 2, Phase 3). The status timeline stepper labels each phase with its number and, for Phase 4, shows the appropriate pathway label based on the category decision (Private Sector, Government Budget, or PPP Transaction Structuring). Every project card across all four review tabs (Intake, FS-1, FS-2, Rejected) now displays the implementing agency below the ministry and the sub-sector below the sector. The same secondary metadata has been added to the project list table and the funding gaps table. Currency values in table columns now use a split prefix/amount format via a new formatCurrencyParts() utility, showing the currency code in muted text and the amount separately for consistent visual alignment. Filter dropdowns on the project list now display short codes next to labels (status codes 1–5, sector codes, origin codes GOV/UNS, pathway codes ODA/PPP/PVS/PVT/DOM). Column headers across the project list use full labels (“Project Title”, “Nominating Ministry”, “Estimated Cost”). On the project detail page, the interactive FS-1 narrative form has been replaced with a read-only FS1NarrativeDisplay component that fetches and renders all five narrative sections. A read-only cash flow projection table is now shown on the feasibility tab when cost data exists. The legacy “Advance to” status button has been removed — all stage transitions now go through the review board. The appraisal wizard action button uses a prominent dark (black) style. The overview section uses simple spacing instead of divider lines. All badge styles for both the FEASIBILITY_STAGE_BADGE_STYLES and PROJECT_STAGE_BADGE_STYLES maps have been normalised to the five-colour system palette (Scarlet, Pale Slate, Blue Slate, Cool Steel, Platinum), eliminating the previous mix of Tailwind greens, purples, indigos, and yellows. The rejected projects API and review board API queries now include implementing_agency and sub_sector fields. A new /api/project-bank/review-board/intake endpoint serves the Intake Kanban columns. A database migration adds the intake_reviews table and new review board stages.

releases.narrativeSummary: This release focuses on two themes: strengthening the Preliminary Feasibility Study workflow and making project data more visible at a glance. The Review Board page has been completely refactored — the monolithic 1,100-line file has been split into four focused tabs: Intake Reviews, FS-1 Reviews, FS-2 Reviews, and a new Rejected Projects tab that shows rejected projects with their rejection reasons and cool-down timers. Each tab is now its own component for maintainability. The project detail page has been substantially expanded: when a project has Preliminary Feasibility Study data, it now displays that data in collapsible sections covering Technical (approach, methodology, risks, design maturity), Revenue (sources, projections, market assessment), Environmental & Social (impact levels with descriptions, land acquisition and resettlement requirements), and Financial Analysis (FIRR, NPV, payback period with inline metric cards). The status timeline has been redesigned from a seven-step model (nominated, screening, appraisal, approved, implementation, completed, rejected) to a clean four-phase stepper (Intake, FS-1, FS-2, FS-3) with completed checkmarks. During FS-1, a sticky Viability Decision sidebar now floats on the right side of the appraisal wizard, showing the routing recommendation (Private, PPP, Government Budget) in real time as the user enters FIRR data. The PFS form now validates that the feasibility study conductor is specified, at least one cost row exists, and environmental and social impact levels are selected before submission. Revenue sources use a multi-select popover with predefined options (toll fees, user charges, lease income, etc.) instead of free-form text, and an “Other” option reveals a description field. Environmental and social impact fields now include description text areas that appear when an impact level is selected. Six new document types have been added — Environmental Impact Assessment, Social Impact Assessment, Land Acquisition Plan, Resettlement Plan, Technical Design, and Market Assessment — and each PFS tab now has its own document upload zone scoped to relevant types. Documents can now be renamed and reclassified inline via a new PATCH endpoint and edit popover. The dashboard has gained count/value toggle buttons on all three charts (pipeline status, pathway, sector), allowing users to switch between viewing project counts and pipeline values. Sector charts now group entries beyond the top five into an “Other” bucket, and tooltips on all charts show both count and value simultaneously. The FS-1 Kanban board columns have been simplified to Submitted, Desk Screened, and Returned (removing the combined Decided column). Sensitivity analysis scenarios now include explanatory tooltips, and revenue projection fields have been moved from the Revenue tab to the Financial Analysis page. The origin cards on the intake form now use background images, and the FundingGapBar uses proportional-width numerals for cleaner alignment.

releases.narrativeSummary: This is a governance-focused release that transforms how projects move through the pipeline. Instead of a flat list of appraisal stages, every project now follows a formal phase-gate model: Intake → FS-1 → FS-2 → FS-3, with the review board acting as a gatekeeper between each phase. When a line ministry finishes filling out an intake form, they click “Submit for Review”, which locks the form and sends it to the review board. Reviewers can approve (unlocking the next phase), return for revision (with comments the submitter can see), or reject. The FS-1 Review Board now supports drag-and-drop — reviewers can drag project cards between Kanban columns — and a gate checklist requires reviewers to verify the narrative, NDP alignment, and cost estimate before passing a project to FS-2. The appraisal wizard has been restructured to match: a redesigned progress rail shows exactly which phase and gate you are at, MSDP screening is now part of the intake form rather than a separate step, and financial analysis (FIRR) is embedded within FS-1. The FIRR page now shows a side-by-side comparison of preliminary cost estimates versus refined figures, with percentage-change indicators and a grouped bar chart. Cash flow entry has been improved with formatted number inputs (commas while typing), quick-entry distribution periods (construction vs operational years), and an integrated chart with annual and cumulative views. The intake form itself has been significantly enhanced: projects can have banner images, origin selection uses illustrated cards instead of a dropdown, ministry and department fields cascade from the chart of accounts, project types and sectors are now managed by administrators through a new admin tab rather than being hardcoded, and regions are grouped by States, Regions, and Union Territories. On the Land Bank side, parcels can now have banner images (starred from the document list), the map supports polyline drawing alongside polygons, location fields use cascading State/Township selects, and NDP goals are selected via clickable cards instead of a dropdown. All sixteen analytics endpoints now exclude internal transfers (pooled fund onward flows) to prevent double-counting, backed by a database-level view update. Data tables across all four modules (Activities, Transactions, Budgets, Planned Disbursements) now use full-width auto-sizing instead of fixed column widths. The entire application has been normalised to a consistent neutral colour palette, replacing the previous mix of blue, purple, and green accents with a cohesive earth-toned scheme.

releases.narrativeSummary: This release adds a complete feasibility study lifecycle to the Project Bank. Every new project now progresses through three stages: FS-1 (Preliminary Screening), where line ministries write a structured narrative covering the problem statement, beneficiaries, NDP alignment, expected outcomes, and cost justification — each section requiring at least 200 words to ensure rigour. Submissions are reviewed on a new Kanban-style Review Board where screening officers process projects through desk review and senior review tiers, with mandatory comments for any rejection or return. A six-month cool-down prevents resubmission of rejected projects from the same ministry. Projects that pass FS-1 move to FS-2 (Detailed Feasibility Study), where a consultant is assigned with a deadline and progress tracking. When the study completes, FIRR and EIRR results are recorded and fed into an algorithmic categorisation engine that recommends Category A (fully private, FIRR ≥ 10%), Category B (government budget), or Category C (PPP, for NDP-aligned projects where FIRR is low but EIRR is strong). Decision-makers can override the recommendation with a mandatory written rationale. PPP structuring has been expanded beyond VGF to include Minimum Revenue Guarantees (with liability caps and trigger conditions) and Availability Payments (with annual amounts and durations). The standalone PPP Pipeline page has been retired — PPP projects are now identified through the categorisation workflow and visible as a column in the main Project List. Across all Project Bank tables, status badges now use a unified monochrome palette, project codes appear as copyable chips, rows feature kebab action menus on hover, and sort columns use arrow icons instead of text characters. The sidebar has been reorganised: “Project Bank” is now “Projects”, the Review Board has its own link, Monitoring is elevated to its own navigation group, and the PPP Pipeline link has been removed.

releases.narrativeSummary: This patch improves the reliability of the Land Bank module. When the system was first deployed, certain reference tables — such as the line ministries lookup and the parcel documents store — might not yet exist in every environment. Previously this could cause the parcel list, parcel detail, and statistics pages to return errors. Now all three Land Bank API endpoints detect when a join fails and automatically retry with a simpler query, so you always see your data even if optional tables haven’t been created yet. The parcel list endpoint also received a refactored filter pipeline for cleaner, more maintainable code.

releases.narrativeSummary: This is a major milestone. The Project Bank is no longer just a submission-and-appraisal pipeline — it now covers the full public-investment lifecycle from nomination through to post-approval monitoring. Projects can specify one of seven PPP contract types aligned with Notification 2/2018 (Availability Payment, BOO, BOT, BTL, BTO, O&M, Other), each with its own detail form covering concession periods, equity ratios, and KPI frameworks. Equity ratio compliance is validated automatically against configurable thresholds (30% for projects under $50M, 20% above). Unsolicited proposals enter through a dedicated origin pathway with proponent details and a Swiss Challenge tab for managing the competitive bidding process. Projects above a configurable dollar threshold cannot reach “approved” status without a cabinet-approval document on file. Once approved, a Monitoring tab appears with scheduled review cadences and report submissions. A new SEE Transfers section tracks State Economic Enterprise equitisations with yearly financials and document uploads. Projects and land parcels are now cross-linked — the project detail sidebar suggests matching parcels, and the parcel detail sidebar suggests matching projects. On the Land Bank side, every parcel now carries a title status (Clear, Pending Verification, Under Review, Disputed, Unregistered), an asset type, a controlling ministry, and NDP goal alignment. Parcel documents can be uploaded and managed from a new Documents tab. The analytics dashboard gains three new chart dimensions: by asset type, by title status, and by ministry. The parcels list page has filters for all new dimensions. Working Groups gain document editing, meeting deletion, and member-count enrichment. The home page now renders inside the standard sidebar layout, and cross-platform routes like FAQ, Build History, and Admin inherit the sidebar context of the module you were last in.

releases.narrativeSummary: This release brings the Land Bank module online and significantly polishes the Project Bank. The Land Bank gets a full analytics dashboard with stat cards, status breakdown, classification pie chart, parcels-by-region bar chart, and a recently-added-parcels table, plus its own sidebar navigation group with items for Dashboard, All Parcels, Register New, Import Parcels, and Analytics. In the Project Bank, the dashboard’s hand-drawn CSS bar charts have been replaced with proper Recharts horizontal bar charts, the project list gains a funding gap progress bar column and a unified filter bar, and the appraisal wizard has been overhauled — every form label now carries a contextual help tooltip, the progress rail shows sub-stage navigation with scroll-to anchors, and the results panels for FIRR and EIRR feature sidebar layouts with hero metric cards, stacked cost/revenue bar charts, and tornado sensitivity-analysis charts. The home page now renders as a standalone full-screen portal (no sidebar wrapper), the module card renamed from “AIMS” to “DFMIS”, and a module switcher has been added to the top-nav user dropdown so you can jump between modules from anywhere. Permissions have been expanded with canCreateParcels, canManageParcels, and canRequestAllocation, with government and development partner roles receiving different land-related capabilities. The land parcels database schema has been overhauled with renamed fields and four new supporting tables.

releases.narrativeSummary: This is the foundation for turning the system from a single AIMS application into a multi-module platform. The sidebar now detects which module you are in based on the URL — /project-bank/* shows the Project Bank navigation, everything else shows the familiar AIMS navigation — and a “Back to Home” link at the top lets you jump between modules. The Project Bank sidebar includes Dashboard, All Projects, Submit New, Funding Gaps, and PPP Pipeline items, with its own “Submit Project” CTA button gated by a new canCreateProjects permission. On the AIMS side, the activity list gains an Origin column that shows whether each activity was donor-reported or came from the Project Bank, displayed as colour-coded badges. Behind the scenes, full TypeScript types have been added for the new database tables: project_bank_projects, project_bank_donors, project_appraisals, project_documents, appraisal_shadow_prices, land_parcels, and national_development_goals. The admin panel gains an NDP Goals tab for managing national development plan goals. The home route now always points to /home (the entry portal) rather than conditionally routing based on organisation assignment, and getCurrentModule() is the new utility that powers the module-aware navigation.

releases.narrativeSummary: The Aid Effectiveness admin panel has been expanded so each government planning document can carry an acronym and a start/end date with configurable precision — year only, month and year, or full date. Those details now appear in the option list and in the Aid Effectiveness survey dropdowns, making it easy to distinguish between overlapping national plans. Pooled fund cards on the Funds page now display the activity’s banner image across the top of the card if one has been set, and the date range at the bottom has been upgraded from a plain text line to a two-column layout with calendar icons. The card is no longer a giant click target — only the title link navigates, which removes all the stopPropagation hacks that were scattered through the card’s event handlers. Three card-view action menus (Activity Card, Organisation Card, and Task Card) that still relied on the third-party bloom-menu library have been replaced with the standard DropdownMenu component used everywhere else in the system, giving them consistent styling and keyboard navigation. The dashboard hero chart-view toggle (bar/line/table) has been updated to use the same subtle slate highlight as the pagination buttons rather than a filled primary button. releases.narrativeSummary: When you opened an activity, the sidebar tabs would all appear empty until you actually clicked into each section and its data finished loading. Now the basic API response includes lightweight record counts for every tab, so completion ticks show up the moment the page loads — no more guessing which sections already have data. The SDG Alignment section, which used to be a sprawling two-column grid of large cards, has been replaced with a compact summary table; clicking any row or an SDG tile opens a dialog where you configure alignment strength, targets, and notes, then close it when you’re done. Contact search now pulls from both the contacts table and the users table so you can find anyone in the system, and it shows results on focus before you start typing. Pagination buttons everywhere — Activities, Transactions, Budgets, Library, FAQ, Build History, and more — now use a subtle slate highlight instead of a filled primary button for the active page. The Focal Points tab has been rebuilt to use the same PersonCard component as the Rolodex, the Humanitarian tab moves the “Add Scope” button into the header area for quicker access, and several analytics charts have had their tooltip and axis formatting tidied up. The fund summary calculation has been corrected so contributions show the most advanced pipeline stage rather than double-counting received plus committed plus pledged amounts. Finally, the dashboard API now excludes internal transfer transactions from totals to prevent inflated figures.

releases.narrativeSummary: This release is about visual consistency and code hygiene. Every dialog and modal in the system — from adding transactions and contacts to editing organisations, tasks, documents, and meetings — now opens with the same muted-background header bar and bottom border, so they all feel like they belong to the same application. Filter labels across analytics pages, the library, admin panels, and user management have been simplified from bold slate headings to smaller, quieter muted text so your eye goes to the data, not the chrome. Behind the scenes, the activity-status display logic that was duplicated in five different files has been pulled into a single shared utility, which means status badges now look identical everywhere — activity cards, the activity editor, and the pooled funds page — and any future status-related change only needs to happen in one place. We also removed the redundant “Story So Far” summary card from the Build History page; the per-release narrative card at the top already tells you everything you need to know.

releases.narrativeSummary: Tables across the system were starting to feel uneven — some columns too wide, others too narrow, and the title column would sometimes get squeezed when lots of columns were visible. We switched every data table (Activities, Transactions, Budgets, and Planned Disbursements) to a fixed-layout approach where each column type gets an appropriate width and the title column stretches to fill whatever space is left, so the layout stays balanced at any screen size. A thin filler column at the right edge ensures that row hover highlights extend all the way to the container boundary. On the Pooled Funds side, the Sankey chart now only shows a third tier of child activities when there are actual disbursement values to display, and hovering over any flow link pops up a tooltip showing the donor or child name and dollar amount. We also moved the fund status badge underneath the IATI identifier for a cleaner card layout, made fund titles clickable links to the fund overview, and swapped the green colour scheme to match the system’s red primary palette. In the sidebar, items previously marked “New” have been moved to the standard under-development indicator for consistency, with only Pooled Funds retaining a “New” badge.

releases.narrativeSummary: We tidied up the tables across æther so they feel more polished and consistent. Previously, the action menu on the activity list used a custom animated component that caused the kebab button to sit out of alignment with the rest of the row — when you hovered over a row the highlight would stop short, leaving an awkward white gap on the right. We replaced that menu with the same straightforward dropdown used elsewhere in the system, removed the now-unnecessary Actions column header from both the Activities and Transactions tables, and made sure that hovering over any row in any table — Activities, Transactions, Budgets, or Planned Disbursements — highlights the entire row edge to edge. Small details, but they add up to a cleaner, more trustworthy interface.
